description: Trace Module 3 — Runner. Executes a saved skill (SKILL.md + skill.meta.json, produced by Module 2 / Synthesizer) on the user's macOS machine by spawning a Claude Sonnet 4.5 agent with the computer_20250124 tool. Pauses before every destructive action to request user confirmation via WebSocket. Implements a global kill-switch that aborts any active run within 2 seconds of hotkey press. Runs as a Python 3.11 FastAPI service mounted at /run on the gateway at 127.0.0.1:8765. The macOS computer-use harness is a custom port of Anthropic's reference Linux/X11 implementation to native Cocoa APIs: CGEventPost for input injection, CGWindowListCreateImage for screenshots, Retina-aware coordinate mapping throughout. Safety architecture has THREE independent layers of destructive-action gating: (1) the SKILL.md file already has ⚠️ markers from the synthesizer, which become per-step prompt instructions; (2) before every tool call, the harness inspects the target AX element's label against the same keyword list from the synthesizer and forces confirmation on match even if the skill didn't flag it; (3) a hard per-run token budget and per-minute action rate limit prevent runaway loops. The agent runtime is abstracted behind an AgentRuntime protocol so OpenAI or Hermes can be swapped in later without rewriting the module. CRITICAL: Ralph iterations on this branch run EXCLUSIVELY in dry-run mode. The `TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1` environment flag gates every code path that would post real events to CGEventPost. Ralph's prompt explicitly forbids setting this flag. The final user story (R-025) is a human-driven live-execution checklist that blocks the PR-to-main, but does NOT block Ralph's completion promise. This split lets Ralph finish autonomously while preventing untested real-world execution from ever reaching main.

techStack.packageManager: uv (shared monorepo workspace with recorder and synthesizer)
techStack.computerUseTool: computer_20250124 (the stable Anthropic computer-use tool schema)
techStack.testing: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx (for mocking anthropic HTTP calls), fakeredis-style in-memory fakes for CGEventPost
techStack.storage: filesystem under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/runs/ + SQLite index shared with recorder/synthesizer

outputStructure.run_metadata.json: run_id (uuid), skill_slug, parameters, started_at, ended_at, status (enum), mode (execute|dry_run), final_step_reached, total_cost_usd, abort_reason (str or null). Schema at contracts/run-metadata.schema.json.
outputStructure.transcript.jsonl: One JSON object per agent turn: {turn: int, role: 'user'|'assistant'|'tool_result', content: <anthropic content blocks>, timestamp_ms, input_tokens_this_turn, output_tokens_this_turn}
outputStructure.events.jsonl: Human-readable trace: {seq, timestamp_ms, type: 'step_start'|'tool_call'|'confirmation_requested'|'confirmed'|'aborted'|'step_complete'|'error', message, step_number, screenshot_ref}
outputStructure.screenshots/NNNN.png: Every screenshot the harness captured during the run (one per agent turn, keyframed)

contracts.destructiveKeywordsSource: IDENTICAL to synthesizer's list in contracts/destructive_keywords.json. Single source of truth — both modules import from that file. Changing the list requires coordinated updates on both branches.

userStories.title: Package scaffold under the uv monorepo workspace
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the services/runner Python package scaffolded under the shared uv workspace with pyproject.toml, mypy strict config, ruff config, pytest config, and the TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE gate wired from day one, so every later story runs quality checks and cannot accidentally drive real input.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/ exists with src layout (src/runner/__init__.py, src/runner/__main__.py, src/runner/api.py placeholder)
Root pyproject.toml's [tool.uv.workspace] members list includes services/runner

services/runner/pyproject.toml declares dependencies: fastapi, uvicorn, anthropic>=0.40, jsonschema, pydantic>=2, pyobjc-framework-Cocoa, pyobjc-framework-Quartz, pyobjc-framework-ApplicationServices, websockets
Dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx, mypy, ruff, pillow (for screenshot validation)
mypy configured for --strict on runner package; pyobjc modules ignored via overrides
ruff config inherits from root (line length 100, target-version py311)
pytest discovers tests in tests/runner/; tests/runner/__init__.py and tests/runner/conftest.py exist
services/runner/src/runner/safety.py exposes is_live_mode_allowed() -> bool that returns os.environ.get('TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE') == '1'
services/runner/src/runner/safety.py also exposes require_live_mode() that raises LiveModeNotAllowed if not allowed, with a message telling the developer this is a safety gate
tests/runner/conftest.py exposes an anthropic_mock fixture using respx AND a monkey-patch fixture that force-sets TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE to unset for every test (opt-in per test to enable it)
tests/runner/test_safety.py: is_live_mode_allowed returns False by default, True when env var is '1', False for any other value including 'true' and '0'
tests/runner/test_safety.py: require_live_mode raises with a clear error message mentioning TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE
services/runner/README.md exists with a prominent SAFETY section listing the three gating layers and the TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE flag rule
uv sync completes without error
uv run ruff check services/runner passes
uv run mypy --strict services/runner passes
uv run pytest tests/runner/ exits 0
userStories.notes: The safety flag plumbing is foundational. No later story is allowed to bypass it. If a future story needs to call CGEventPost, it MUST first call require_live_mode() or route through the dry-run fake. Do NOT add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY anywhere in code — env only.

userStories.title: run_metadata.json JSON schema and model
userStories.description: As the runner module, I need a locked JSON schema for run_metadata.json so UI, tests, and post-run analysis have a stable contract for run artifacts.
acceptanceCriteria: contracts/run-metadata.schema.json exists at repo root as a valid JSON Schema draft 2020-12
Required fields: run_id (uuid string), skill_slug (string), started_at (date-time), ended_at (date-time or null), status (enum from contracts.runStatusEnum), mode (enum: execute, dry_run)

Optional fields: parameters (object, flat string-keyed map), final_step_reached (int or null), total_cost_usd (number), abort_reason (string or null), input_tokens_total (int), output_tokens_total (int), error_message (string or null), confirmation_count (int), destructive_actions_executed (array of int step numbers)
additionalProperties: false at the top level
Status enum: pending, running, awaiting_confirmation, succeeded, failed, aborted, budget_exceeded, rate_limited
services/runner/src/runner/schema.py exposes load_run_metadata_schema(), validate_run_metadata(obj), and a RunMetadata pydantic model mirroring the schema for in-memory use
RunMetadata model has a to_dict() that emits schema-valid JSON and a from_dict() classmethod that raises ValidationError on malformed input
RunMetadata includes an is_terminal() method returning True if status in {succeeded, failed, aborted, budget_exceeded, rate_limited}
RunMetadata includes an is_waiting() method returning True if status == awaiting_confirmation
tests/runner/test_schema.py: 5+ valid example metadata objects pass validation
tests/runner/test_schema.py: 10+ malformed examples fail with useful errors (bad status, bad UUID, unknown field, missing required, wrong type)
tests/runner/test_schema.py: RunMetadata model round-trips (dict → model → dict produces identical output)
tests/runner/test_schema.py: is_terminal() and is_waiting() return correct values for each status
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Locked once shipped. Keep additionalProperties:false on the top level — catches typos like 'cost_usd' vs 'total_cost_usd' that would otherwise silently drift.

userStories.title: Shared destructive keywords source of truth
userStories.description: As both the synthesizer and runner modules, we need ONE file that defines destructive keywords, so the list cannot drift between modules. The synthesizer already uses this list; the runner imports from the same file.
acceptanceCriteria: contracts/destructive_keywords.json exists at repo root as a JSON array of lowercase strings (the 14 keywords from the description)
services/runner/src/runner/destructive.py exposes load_destructive_keywords() -> frozenset[str] that reads the JSON file and returns a frozenset for O(1) membership
services/runner/src/runner/destructive.py exposes matches_destructive_keyword(text: str) -> bool that does case-insensitive word-boundary matching (using re.search with \b)
matches_destructive_keyword handles text with punctuation, leading/trailing whitespace, multiple words, and returns True if ANY keyword is found
matches_destructive_keyword returns False for substring matches that aren't word boundaries (e.g., 'sender' does NOT match 'send')
matches_destructive_keyword normalizes unicode whitespace and fullwidth characters using unicodedata.normalize before matching
Synthesizer's destructive_matcher (from S-008) is refactored to import from the same file — if it doesn't already, update in this story
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: each of the 14 keywords matches as a word boundary
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: 'sender', 'postpone', 'submitted' do NOT match
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: 'Click Send' (multi-word) matches
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: 'SEND' (uppercase) matches (case-insensitive)
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: empty string returns False
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: None input raises TypeError (don't silently return False)
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: unicode label with fullwidth chars normalizes correctly
tests/runner/test_destructive.py: load_destructive_keywords reads the JSON file and returns a frozenset
scripts/check_contracts.sh verifies the JSON array has exactly 14 entries, all lowercase (asserting the list hasn't been accidentally expanded without coordination)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This story has a cross-branch dependency: if S-008 shipped with a hardcoded list in the synthesizer, we DEDUPLICATE here and update the synthesizer to import from the shared file. Document the coordination in progress.txt.

userStories.title: Skill loader: parse SKILL.md + meta + parameter substitution
userStories.description: As the runner, I need a SkillLoader that loads a skill from disk, parses it using the synthesizer's skill_doc module, substitutes user-supplied parameters into step text, and validates the result, so downstream execution operates on a concrete step list.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/skill_loader.py exposes load_skill(slug: str, skills_root: Path) -> LoadedSkill where LoadedSkill has {parsed_skill: ParsedSkill, meta: dict, skill_path: Path}
Raises SkillNotFoundError if the skill directory doesn't exist
Validates meta against contracts/skill-meta.schema.json; raises SkillIntegrityError on failure
Validates markdown+meta cross-consistency using synthesizer.schema.validate_meta_against_markdown; raises SkillIntegrityError on failure
Uses synthesizer's skill_doc.parse_skill_md to parse SKILL.md (imports from the synthesizer package via uv workspace, does NOT copy-paste the parser)
exposes substitute_parameters(skill: LoadedSkill, params: dict[str, str]) -> LoadedSkill that returns a new LoadedSkill with {param_name} replaced in every step's text
substitute_parameters validates that every REQUIRED parameter in meta.parameters has a value supplied; raises MissingParameterError with the parameter name if not
substitute_parameters rejects extra parameters not declared in meta.parameters (raises UnknownParameterError with the name)
substitute_parameters uses defaults from meta.parameters when optional parameters are omitted
substitute_parameters handles escaped braces (\{literal}) correctly — they remain literal
substitute_parameters preserves step numbering and destructive flags — only step text is mutated
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: loads each of the 5 golden fixture skills, asserts all pass validation
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: skill with a mismatched ⚠️ flag vs meta.destructive_steps → SkillIntegrityError
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: substitution with all params → step text has no remaining {param} references
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: substitution with missing required param → MissingParameterError with the name
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: substitution with unknown param → UnknownParameterError
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: substitution with only optional params (defaults applied) → succeeds
tests/runner/test_skill_loader.py: skill directory missing skill.meta.json → SkillNotFoundError or SkillIntegrityError with a clear message
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is where the Runner first depends on the Synthesizer's merged code. If parse_skill_md changes on the synthesizer side, this module breaks at integration time — that's the point of the contract.

userStories.title: Run artifact writer with atomic metadata updates and screenshot storage
userStories.description: As the runner, I need a RunWriter that creates the run directory, writes run_metadata.json atomically on every status change, appends events and transcript entries as they happen, and stores screenshots by seq, so a run's state on disk is always consistent even if the process crashes mid-run.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/run_writer.py exposes RunWriter class
Constructor takes a run_id, skill_slug, mode, and runs_root directory; creates the run directory with 0700 perms
write_metadata(metadata: RunMetadata) writes run_metadata.json atomically: write .tmp → fsync → rename
update_status(status: str, **fields) updates metadata with the new status and any additional fields, then writes atomically
append_event(event_type: str, message: str, step_number: int|None, screenshot_seq: int|None) appends one JSON line to events.jsonl and flushes
append_transcript(turn: int, role: str, content: list, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) appends to transcript.jsonl and flushes
write_screenshot(seq: int, png_bytes: bytes) writes screenshots/NNNN.png where NNNN is zero-padded 4 digits
close() finalizes ended_at and writes final metadata
Is thread-safe: a single RLock guards all writes
Metadata writer is idempotent — calling update_status with the same values twice is safe (no duplicate writes, same file contents)
If metadata write fails (disk full, perms), the exception propagates but does NOT leave a .tmp file behind
Screenshot write validates PNG magic bytes before writing; raises ValueError with a helpful message if invalid
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: full lifecycle (create → update_status → 10 events + 5 screenshots + 3 transcript turns → close) produces a readable run directory
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: concurrent append from 3 threads produces 30 events with monotonic seq
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: simulated crash (mock fsync to raise after .tmp write) leaves the .tmp file removed and the existing run_metadata.json intact
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: invalid PNG bytes → ValueError
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: close() is idempotent — calling twice is safe
tests/runner/test_run_writer.py: run directory perms are 0700
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Atomic writes matter here because a crashed run's metadata must still be readable — we show it as 'aborted' or 'failed' in the UI. Partial writes that corrupt the metadata file would orphan runs.

userStories.title: Retina-aware coordinate mapping and screenshot normalization
userStories.description: As the harness, when I take a screenshot for Claude and Claude returns pixel coordinates for a click, I need to correctly map those coordinates back to screen points on a Retina display, so clicks land on the actual UI elements.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/coords.py exposes DisplayInfo dataclass with {width_points, height_points, scale_factor, width_pixels, height_pixels}
get_main_display_info() -> DisplayInfo queries NSScreen.mainScreen() for logical size and backingScaleFactor
points_to_pixels(x_pt: float, y_pt: float, info: DisplayInfo) -> tuple[int, int] and pixels_to_points(x_px: int, y_px: int, info: DisplayInfo) -> tuple[float, float]

capture_and_normalize(info: DisplayInfo, target_longest_edge: int = 1568) -> tuple[bytes, ImageMapping] captures a full screenshot and optionally downscales so the longest edge is target_longest_edge (matches Claude's recommended max for computer use)

Returns png bytes AND an ImageMapping object with {original_pixels, resized_pixels, scale_from_resized_to_points} so coordinates from Claude (in the resized image's coord space) can be mapped back to screen points
resized_pixels_to_points(x, y, mapping: ImageMapping) -> tuple[float, float] does the inverse mapping
Downscaling preserves aspect ratio; uses Pillow's LANCZOS filter
Exposes a DryRunDisplayInfo constant that mimics a common Retina display (2880x1800 pixels, 1440x900 points, scale 2.0) for dry-run and test use
tests/runner/test_coords.py: points ↔ pixels round-trip for a 2.0-scale display
tests/runner/test_coords.py: points ↔ pixels round-trip for a 1.0-scale (non-Retina) display
tests/runner/test_coords.py: resized_pixels_to_points on a 1568x980 resize of a 2880x1800 screenshot maps correctly to points
tests/runner/test_coords.py: capture_and_normalize returns valid PNG bytes (verified with Pillow)
tests/runner/test_coords.py: target_longest_edge of a tiny (800x600) screenshot — no upscaling, returned bytes are the original
tests/runner/test_coords.py: off-by-one precision — a click at the exact pixel center of a 100x100 button maps back to the button's center in points
Integration-ish test: @pytest.mark.macos test that captures a real screenshot and verifies DisplayInfo matches the actual display (human tester can run this on their machine; skipped in CI)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: THIS IS THE SINGLE MOST FAILURE-PRONE PART OF THE MODULE. Retina off-by-2x errors are the single most likely class of bug — get the tests right here or misclicks will be a mystery later. The 1568px target longest-edge is Anthropic's documented recommendation for computer use.

userStories.title: Dry-run input adapter: fake CGEventPost and screenshot-from-trajectory
userStories.description: As the runner in dry-run mode (and in Ralph iterations), I need an input adapter that accepts click/type/scroll commands but does NOT post real events, and a screenshot source that returns synthetic screenshots (from the original trajectory) instead of live screen captures, so Ralph can test the full execution loop without driving real input.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/input_adapter.py exposes an InputAdapter protocol with methods: click(x, y, button, modifiers), type_text(text), key_press(keys), scroll(x, y, direction, amount), move_mouse(x, y)
services/runner/src/runner/input_adapter.py exposes DryRunInputAdapter that implements InputAdapter but does nothing except record the call into a list for inspection
DryRunInputAdapter exposes a get_recorded_calls() -> list[tuple] that returns all recorded calls in order
services/runner/src/runner/screen_source.py exposes a ScreenSource protocol with method capture() -> tuple[bytes, ImageMapping]

services/runner/src/runner/screen_source.py exposes TrajectoryScreenSource(trajectory_id: str) that returns screenshots from the original trajectory's screenshots/ directory in sequence — useful when dry-running a skill whose source trajectory still exists
TrajectoryScreenSource falls back to a static 'blank canvas' PNG if the trajectory screenshot is missing
DryRunInputAdapter and TrajectoryScreenSource explicitly do NOT call is_live_mode_allowed() — they are safe to use regardless of the flag
services/runner/src/runner/input_adapter.py's constructor for LiveInputAdapter (implemented in X-008) calls require_live_mode() in its __init__ — instantiation fails if TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE is not set
tests/runner/test_input_adapter.py: DryRunInputAdapter records click, type_text, key_press, scroll, move_mouse in order, with correct arguments
tests/runner/test_input_adapter.py: DryRunInputAdapter can be cleared and reused (clear() method)
tests/runner/test_screen_source.py: TrajectoryScreenSource with a valid trajectory returns consecutive screenshots on capture() calls
tests/runner/test_screen_source.py: TrajectoryScreenSource with a missing screenshot falls back to the blank canvas
tests/runner/test_screen_source.py: TrajectoryScreenSource with a missing trajectory directory → FileNotFoundError with a clear message
tests/runner/test_input_adapter.py: attempting to instantiate LiveInputAdapter with TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE unset raises LiveModeNotAllowed
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The dry-run adapter is how Ralph iterates safely. Every test in this module uses DryRunInputAdapter by default. The LiveInputAdapter is written in X-008 and is the ONLY place that calls CGEventPost.

userStories.title: Live macOS input adapter using CGEventPost
userStories.description: As the runner in live mode, I need a LiveInputAdapter that posts real mouse and keyboard events via CGEventPost, correctly maps Retina coordinates, and respects the TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE gate, so actual workflow replay can happen on a real machine.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/live_input.py exposes LiveInputAdapter implementing InputAdapter
__init__ calls require_live_mode() FIRST — raises LiveModeNotAllowed if TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE != '1'
__init__ stores a DisplayInfo captured at construction time (for coordinate mapping)

click(x_pt, y_pt, button, modifiers): posts CGEventCreateMouseEvent(kCGEventLeftMouseDown/Up or Right equivalent) at the point coordinates (CGEventPost uses points on macOS, not pixels — verify this in tests)
click respects modifier flags (cmd, shift, option, control) using CGEventSetFlags
type_text(text): uses CGEventKeyboardSetUnicodeString to synthesize Unicode-aware typing; supports emoji and non-ASCII
key_press(keys): for shortcut combinations like ['cmd', 'n']; posts key-down events in order, then key-up in reverse order
scroll(x, y, direction, amount): uses CGEventCreateScrollWheelEvent
move_mouse(x, y): uses CGEventCreateMouseEvent with kCGEventMouseMoved
Adds a small delay (20 ms default, configurable) between posted events to avoid overwhelming the target app — prevents lost events
After every posted event, polls for CGEventTapDisabled and logs a warning if the system has throttled us
Each method wraps its CGEventPost call in a try/except and re-raises with a clear 'LiveInputError: <op> failed: <reason>' so errors don't surface as cryptic PyObjC tracebacks
tests/runner/test_live_input.py: instantiation without TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE raises LiveModeNotAllowed

tests/runner/test_live_input.py: with TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE set (in a test-only fixture) AND on macOS, a click posts an event that a secondary event tap can observe — @pytest.mark.macos and @pytest.mark.live_input, SKIPPED by default
tests/runner/test_live_input.py: modifier flags test (post cmd+click, verify flags)
tests/runner/test_live_input.py: Unicode text input (post 'héllo 👋', verify via secondary tap)
tests/runner/test_live_input.py: mock-based tests that verify the PyObjC call signatures without actually posting (most tests use this mode — cheap, deterministic)
services/runner/README.md's SAFETY section is updated with a warning that ANY test using this adapter requires explicit opt-in AND should run on a disposable test machine, not production
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes (skipping the @pytest.mark.live_input tests by default)
userStories.notes: The CGEventPost API uses POINTS, not pixels — this is why X-006 exists. Ralph iterations will NEVER run the @pytest.mark.live_input tests because they require the TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE flag, which Ralph's prompt forbids. Human live testing (X-025) is where these get exercised.

userStories.title: Live screen source using CGWindowListCreateImage
userStories.description: As the runner in live mode, I need a LiveScreenSource that captures real screenshots of the main display with Retina-correct output, so Claude sees what's actually on screen.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/live_screen.py exposes LiveScreenSource implementing ScreenSource
__init__ calls require_live_mode()
capture() -> tuple[bytes, ImageMapping]: uses CGWindowListCreateImage with kCGNullWindowID, kCGWindowListOptionOnScreenOnly, kCGWindowImageDefault
Converts the resulting CGImage to PNG bytes via NSBitmapImageRep
Downscales the screenshot to longest-edge 1568 using coords.capture_and_normalize (re-uses X-006)
Returns the ImageMapping so Claude-space coordinates can be mapped back
Captures completed within 200 ms on a typical Retina display
Logs a warning if capture takes longer than 500 ms
Handles the case where Screen Recording permission is denied — raises PermissionDeniedError with a clear message telling the user to grant permission in System Settings
tests/runner/test_live_screen.py: without TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE → LiveModeNotAllowed
tests/runner/test_live_screen.py (marked @pytest.mark.macos): with permission, capture() returns valid PNG bytes at the expected resized dimensions
tests/runner/test_live_screen.py: mocked permission denial raises PermissionDeniedError
tests/runner/test_live_screen.py: mocked capture timeout (> 500 ms) logs a warning but still returns
Mock-based tests verify PyObjC call signatures without requiring real macOS APIs for the Ralph loop
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Works with the same Screen Recording permission as the Recorder. If the user granted it during Module 1 setup, it carries over to Module 3.

userStories.title: AgentRuntime protocol and ClaudeRuntime implementation
userStories.description: As the runner, I need an AgentRuntime protocol abstraction with a ClaudeRuntime implementation that calls the Anthropic API with the computer_20250124 tool, so the agent runtime is swappable (future OpenAI/Hermes) and unit-testable.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/agent_runtime.py exposes an AgentRuntime protocol with method: async run_turn(system_prompt: str, messages: list[Message], max_tokens: int = 4096) -> AgentResponse
AgentResponse is a dataclass with {content_blocks (list of Anthropic content), stop_reason, input_tokens, output_tokens, turn_number}
services/runner/src/runner/claude_runtime.py exposes ClaudeRuntime implementing AgentRuntime

ClaudeRuntime calls anthropic.AsyncAnthropic's messages.create with model=claude-sonnet-4-5 and tools=[{type: 'computer_20250124', name: 'computer', display_width_px: ..., display_height_px: ..., display_number: 1}]
Display dimensions are passed from the ImageMapping of the most recent screenshot — if the first turn has no prior screenshot yet, uses DryRunDisplayInfo's resized dimensions as a sensible default
Retries on 429/5xx with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s; max 5 retries) — same policy as synthesizer LLMClient
Fails fast on 401 with a clear ANTHROPIC_API_KEY message
Logs cost per turn to costs.jsonl: {timestamp, module: 'runner', model, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_estimate_usd, run_id}
Supports fake mode via TRACE_LLM_FAKE_MODE=1 — returns canned responses from fixtures/llm_responses/runner_*.json based on input hash
Fake-mode canned responses cover all 5 reference workflows — each has a multi-turn conversation scripted (tool_use → tool_result → tool_use → ... → end_turn)
Exposes a stub OpenAIRuntime class that raises NotImplementedError — demonstrates the swap seam but doesn't implement it
tests/runner/test_agent_runtime.py (fake mode): each of the 5 canned scripts runs through successfully, returning the expected sequence of tool_use and end_turn stop_reasons
tests/runner/test_agent_runtime.py: 429 retry test via respx
tests/runner/test_agent_runtime.py: 401 fails fast without retry
tests/runner/test_agent_runtime.py: cost logging appends the right fields to costs.jsonl
tests/runner/test_agent_runtime.py: OpenAIRuntime instantiation raises NotImplementedError
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The fake-mode canned scripts are the test oracle for the entire execution loop. They must be realistic — include screenshots in the tool_result content blocks, include realistic step-by-step tool_use blocks, and include confirmation requests (via <needs_confirmation> tags in the text blocks). These canned scripts are where the Ralph loop really gets tested.

userStories.title: Execution system prompt and step renderer
userStories.description: As the agent, I need a system prompt that explains the workflow structure (numbered steps, destructive markers, parameter substitution, confirmation protocol, completion signals), so the LLM behaves predictably across runs and workflows.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/execution_prompt.py exposes build_execution_prompt(loaded_skill: LoadedSkill, mode: str) -> str

The prompt includes: (1) a preamble explaining the task (execute the workflow, one step at a time, verify each step), (2) the full resolved SKILL.md (parameters already substituted) in a fenced section, (3) the destructive-step protocol

Destructive-step protocol in prompt: 'For any step marked ⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE], DO NOT use the computer tool to execute it. Instead, emit EXACTLY this text: <needs_confirmation step="N"/> where N is the step number. Then stop your turn. When confirmed, you will receive a user message allowing you to proceed.'
Completion protocol: 'When all steps are done, emit <workflow_complete/> and stop.'
Error protocol: 'If you cannot proceed (app not in expected state, UI missing, screen unreadable), emit <workflow_failed reason="..."/> and stop.'
Verification protocol: 'After each click/type action, take a screenshot with the computer tool before moving on.'
The prompt is deterministic — same LoadedSkill produces byte-identical prompt (important for caching + fake-mode hashing)
In dry_run mode, prompt has an extra line: 'This is a DRY RUN. Your tool calls will be received but no real input will be posted.' — purely informational, doesn't change protocol
tests/runner/test_execution_prompt.py: builds a prompt for each of the 5 fixture skills; asserts structural sections present
tests/runner/test_execution_prompt.py: determinism — building the same prompt twice is byte-identical
tests/runner/test_execution_prompt.py: dry_run extra line present only in dry_run mode
tests/runner/test_execution_prompt.py: destructive-step protocol is present verbatim (catches accidental prompt edits that could change agent behavior)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The prompt is load-bearing for safety. The literal string '<needs_confirmation step="N"/>' is what the executor parses — if the prompt drifts and the model emits something slightly different, the parser won't match and a destructive action could slip through. The harness-layer keyword matcher (X-015) is the backup for this case.

userStories.title: Tool call parser and confirmation tag detector
userStories.description: As the execution loop, when the agent emits a response, I need to parse tool_use blocks, detect <needs_confirmation step="N"/> and <workflow_complete/> and <workflow_failed reason="..."/> tags, and return a structured Action for the executor to dispatch on, so the main loop stays simple.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/parser.py exposes parse_agent_response(response: AgentResponse) -> ParsedAction

ParsedAction is a tagged union: ToolCallAction (tool_name, tool_input, tool_use_id), ConfirmationRequest (step_number), WorkflowComplete, WorkflowFailed (reason), UnknownAction (raw_text) for when the agent emits plain text we don't recognize
Extracts <needs_confirmation step="N"/> from any text block; step N must be a positive integer, else UnknownAction
Extracts <workflow_complete/> and <workflow_failed reason="..."/> with XML-ish attribute parsing that handles quoted strings

When the response has BOTH a tool_use block AND a <needs_confirmation> tag, returns ConfirmationRequest and IGNORES the tool_use (safety-first — a confirmation signal means the agent intended to stop, so we drop the pending action)
When the response has a tool_use block for the computer tool, returns ToolCallAction with tool_input parsed
When the response has a text block with none of the special tags, returns UnknownAction (the executor logs it and treats it as a no-op turn)
When the response's stop_reason is 'end_turn' with no special tags, returns UnknownAction (likely the agent finished without signaling — main loop treats this as a failure)
Handles malformed tags gracefully (missing quotes, missing step number) — returns UnknownAction with the raw text, never raises
tests/runner/test_parser.py: tool_use block → ToolCallAction with tool_name='computer' and tool_input dict
tests/runner/test_parser.py: text with <needs_confirmation step="5"/> → ConfirmationRequest(5)
tests/runner/test_parser.py: text with <workflow_complete/> → WorkflowComplete
tests/runner/test_parser.py: text with <workflow_failed reason="app not found"/> → WorkflowFailed('app not found')
tests/runner/test_parser.py: BOTH tool_use AND <needs_confirmation> present → returns ConfirmationRequest, ignores the tool_use
tests/runner/test_parser.py: malformed <needs_confirmation> (missing quotes, non-integer step, extra attributes) → UnknownAction
tests/runner/test_parser.py: <needs_confirmation step="0"/> (non-positive) → UnknownAction
tests/runner/test_parser.py: whitespace tolerance — '< needs_confirmation step="3" />' (with spaces) also matches
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The 'BOTH tool_use AND needs_confirmation → use the confirmation' rule is the first layer of the belt-and-suspenders destructive gate. The LLM might emit a tool_use to click Send AND emit <needs_confirmation> in the same turn — we ALWAYS honor the confirmation tag.

userStories.title: Computer tool action dispatcher (maps Claude tool calls to input adapter)
userStories.description: As the execution loop, given a ToolCallAction with the computer tool input, I need a dispatcher that translates Claude's tool_input (actions like 'screenshot', 'left_click', 'type', 'key', 'scroll', 'mouse_move') into InputAdapter method calls and ScreenSource capture, so the agent's abstract tool calls become concrete machine actions.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/dispatcher.py exposes dispatch_tool_call(action: ToolCallAction, input_adapter: InputAdapter, screen_source: ScreenSource, image_mapping: ImageMapping) -> ToolResult
ToolResult is a dataclass with {content_blocks (for feeding back to Claude), new_image_mapping (after screenshots change)}
Handles tool_input.action values per the computer_20250124 tool spec: 'screenshot', 'left_click', 'right_click', 'double_click', 'middle_click', 'type', 'key', 'scroll', 'mouse_move', 'wait'
'screenshot': calls screen_source.capture(), returns an image content block with base64 PNG and updates image_mapping

'left_click' with coordinate [x_resized, y_resized]: maps to points via image_mapping, calls input_adapter.click(x_pt, y_pt, button='left', modifiers=[]), then takes a fresh screenshot as verification
'right_click', 'double_click', 'middle_click': same pattern as left_click with appropriate button/click count
'type' with text: calls input_adapter.type_text(text), then screenshot
'key' with keys string like 'cmd+n' or 'Return': parses into modifier list + base key, calls input_adapter.key_press([...]), then screenshot
'scroll' with coordinate and direction ('up'/'down'/'left'/'right') and amount: maps coord to points, calls input_adapter.scroll(x_pt, y_pt, direction, amount), then screenshot
'mouse_move' with coordinate: maps to points, calls input_adapter.move_mouse(x_pt, y_pt), NO screenshot after (mouse move doesn't change screen state)
'wait' with duration: sleeps for the specified seconds (capped at 10s for safety), then screenshot
Unknown action name: returns a tool_result with is_error=true and message 'Unknown computer action: <name>'
Actions that require coordinates but receive bad coords (missing, negative, out of bounds) return an error tool_result without calling the adapter
After every action that changes screen state (click, type, key, scroll, wait), the screenshot is taken BEFORE returning and included in the returned content blocks
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: each of the 10 action types is dispatched correctly with DryRunInputAdapter and TrajectoryScreenSource
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: coordinate mapping — a click at resized [100, 100] in a 1568x980 image on a 2880x1800 display dispatches to roughly [91.8, 91.8] points (with 0.1 tolerance)
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: 'key' parsing — 'cmd+shift+s' parses to ['cmd', 'shift', 's']
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: unknown action returns error tool_result, does not raise
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: wait is capped at 10s even if the agent requests 60s
tests/runner/test_dispatcher.py: screenshot is NOT taken after mouse_move
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The wait cap is a defense against an agent that decides to sleep for a day. The coordinate mapping is where X-006 either pays off or burns us.

userStories.title: Run budget and rate limit enforcement
userStories.description: As the runner, I need per-run token budgets, per-run wall-clock time limits, and a per-minute action rate limit, so a runaway agent can't spiral into cost or actions far beyond what a skill should take.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/budget.py exposes RunBudget dataclass with {max_input_tokens, max_output_tokens, max_wall_clock_seconds, max_actions_per_minute, max_total_actions}
Default RunBudget: max_input_tokens=500000, max_output_tokens=50000, max_wall_clock_seconds=600 (10 min), max_actions_per_minute=30, max_total_actions=100
RunBudget can be customized per-skill via an optional skill.meta.json field 'runtime_limits' (schema updated if needed — coordinate via X-002 if it's not already optional)
services/runner/src/runner/budget.py exposes BudgetTracker class initialized with a RunBudget
BudgetTracker.record_turn(input_tokens, output_tokens) — accumulates totals
BudgetTracker.record_action() — increments action count and timestamps for rate limit window
BudgetTracker.check() -> BudgetStatus — returns one of OK, BUDGET_EXCEEDED (with reason), RATE_LIMITED (with remaining wait seconds)
Rate limit window is a sliding 60-second window
Wall clock check uses time.monotonic() from the start of the run
When BUDGET_EXCEEDED, the reason enumerates which specific limit was hit (tokens/time/actions)
When RATE_LIMITED, the executor sleeps for the specified duration rather than aborting — this is a soft limit
tests/runner/test_budget.py: default budget creation
tests/runner/test_budget.py: token limit triggered by cumulative token counts across multiple turns
tests/runner/test_budget.py: wall-clock limit triggered via time.monotonic mocking (use freezegun or monkeypatch)
tests/runner/test_budget.py: action rate limit — 30 actions in 1 second → RATE_LIMITED with remaining wait
tests/runner/test_budget.py: total actions limit — 100 actions → BUDGET_EXCEEDED reason 'actions'
tests/runner/test_budget.py: rate limit sliding window — 29 actions in one second, then 1 more action 61 seconds later → OK
tests/runner/test_budget.py: custom RunBudget from skill.meta.json runtime_limits field is honored
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The rate limit is a SOFT limit — we sleep rather than abort. This is because a well-behaved agent might burst actions during a short sequence (click → verify → click → verify). The HARD limits are the token and wall-clock caps — those abort the run.

userStories.title: Pre-action destructive keyword matcher (harness-layer gate)
userStories.description: As the third layer of destructive-action protection, before dispatching any click-class tool call, I need to query the AX element at the target coordinates, match its label against destructive keywords, and force a confirmation pause if matched — even if the skill didn't flag this step, even if Claude didn't emit <needs_confirmation>, so no destructive action reaches the live input adapter without explicit user approval.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/pre_action_gate.py exposes inspect_click_target(x_pt: float, y_pt: float, ax_resolver: AXResolver) -> GateDecision
GateDecision is a union: AllowAction, RequireConfirmation(label, reason), Unknown (AX didn't return a target — treated as AllowAction but logged)
Reuses the recorder's ax_resolver module via uv workspace import — does NOT copy-paste resolution logic
If the resolved AX target's label matches destructive keywords (via matches_destructive_keyword from X-003), returns RequireConfirmation
If the AX target's role is 'AXButton' with a destructive keyword in label, RequireConfirmation
If the AX target's role is 'AXLink' with a destructive keyword, RequireConfirmation (e.g. 'Delete Account' link)
If AX resolution times out or returns no target, returns Unknown — the executor's policy is to ALLOW this (do not over-block on imperfect AX) but logs it
Is a no-op in dry_run mode — dry_run always returns AllowAction since no real click will happen (avoids false positives from trajectory screenshots)

Exposes apply_gate_to_tool_call(action: ToolCallAction, image_mapping: ImageMapping, ax_resolver: AXResolver, mode: str) -> GateDecision that handles coordinate mapping and only gates click-class actions (not type, scroll, mouse_move, wait, screenshot)
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: click on an AX target with label 'Send' → RequireConfirmation
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: click on an AX target with label 'Cancel' → AllowAction
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: click with AX resolution returning None → Unknown → executor allows (policy test)
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: type action is never gated (always AllowAction)
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: dry_run mode always returns AllowAction even with a destructive-label target
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: AX target with role 'AXStaticText' and destructive keyword → AllowAction (gate only fires on actionable elements: AXButton, AXLink, AXMenuItem, AXCheckBox)
tests/runner/test_pre_action_gate.py: AX label with 'Delete' in non-English context — matches (English-only v1)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the third and strictest destructive gate. The first two (skill ⚠️ markers + <needs_confirmation> tag) depend on the LLM doing the right thing. This one does NOT — it inspects the actual screen state at action time. If the LLM somehow emits a tool_use to click Send and doesn't flag it, this gate catches it. Live mode only — in dry run we trust the skill's flags.

userStories.title: Confirmation queue and WebSocket protocol
userStories.description: As the runner when it hits a destructive step, I need to pause execution, push a confirmation request to a queue, expose it via WebSocket to the UI, and wait for the user's confirm or abort decision before resuming, so the user has a clear gate.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/confirmation.py exposes ConfirmationQueue class
ConfirmationQueue is backed by asyncio.Queue per run_id
push_request(run_id, step_number, step_text, screenshot_ref, destructive_reason) adds a ConfirmationRequest to the queue and transitions the run's status to awaiting_confirmation

await_decision(run_id, timeout_seconds=300) -> ConfirmationDecision waits for the UI to call submit_decision; default 5-minute timeout; on timeout returns ConfirmationDecision(action='abort', reason='user_timeout')
submit_decision(run_id, decision) called from the API side when the UI responds — decision is 'confirm' or 'abort'
Only one pending confirmation per run at a time; pushing a second request while one is pending raises IllegalState
WebSocket message format for UI → runner: {type: 'confirmation_response', run_id, decision: 'confirm'|'abort', reason?: string}
WebSocket message format for runner → UI: {type: 'confirmation_request', run_id, step_number, step_text, destructive_reason, screenshot_url}
Executor calls await_decision and acts on the result: 'confirm' → dispatch the held tool call; 'abort' → transition to aborted with reason
Kill switch (X-018) also pushes an abort decision to any pending queue for that run_id
tests/runner/test_confirmation.py: full confirm/abort flow (push → await → submit → await returns decision)
tests/runner/test_confirmation.py: timeout test (push → no submit → await returns abort with reason='user_timeout')
tests/runner/test_confirmation.py: double-push raises IllegalState
tests/runner/test_confirmation.py: submit_decision for a run with no pending request is a no-op (warning logged)
tests/runner/test_confirmation.py: kill_run pushes abort decision to any pending await
WebSocket integration test (X-019 covers this end-to-end) — this story only tests the queue
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The 5-minute timeout on confirmation is deliberately short — if the user has walked away from their machine, the run should abort rather than sit open forever with the screenshot stale. UI must surface confirmations immediately with sound + notification.

userStories.title: Main execution loop
userStories.description: As the runner, I need the core execution loop that ties together the LLM runtime, parser, dispatcher, pre-action gate, budget tracker, confirmation queue, and run writer, so the agent actually executes skills from start to finish.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/executor.py exposes Executor class
Executor(loaded_skill, parameters, mode, agent_runtime, input_adapter, screen_source, ax_resolver, budget, writer, confirmation_queue, run_id)
async run() -> RunMetadata: orchestrates the full run
Turn loop: (1) build messages (system prompt + prior turns), (2) call agent_runtime.run_turn, (3) budget.record_turn + check → maybe abort, (4) parse response, (5) dispatch on action type

ToolCallAction dispatch: (a) if click-class, run pre_action_gate → if RequireConfirmation, force a ConfirmationRequest and wait, (b) budget.record_action + check, (c) dispatcher.dispatch_tool_call, (d) append tool_result to messages
ConfirmationRequest dispatch: push to queue, await_decision, on confirm → log + allow the NEXT tool_use that matches the step (agent will retry), on abort → end run with status=aborted
WorkflowComplete: end run with status=succeeded
WorkflowFailed: end run with status=failed, error_message from the reason

UnknownAction: append a user message 'Your response did not include a tool call or known tag; please continue or emit <workflow_complete/>'; increment unknown_action_count; if >3 in a row, abort with status=failed reason='agent_stuck'
Every turn: writer.append_transcript, writer.append_event
Every tool call: writer.append_event with step_number tracked from the skill's current step
Every screenshot: writer.write_screenshot with sequential seq
Budget breach: writer.update_status(budget_exceeded, ...), return
Exception in any phase: writer.update_status(failed, error_message=str(e)), re-raise so the API sees it
Before first turn: writer.write_metadata initial with status=running, started_at set
tests/runner/test_executor.py (fake mode): happy path — run each of the 5 reference skills to completion, assert final status=succeeded, step_count matches skill
tests/runner/test_executor.py: confirmation flow — agent emits <needs_confirmation> on step 3, test submits 'confirm', executor proceeds and completes
tests/runner/test_executor.py: abort flow — agent emits <needs_confirmation>, test submits 'abort', executor ends with status=aborted
tests/runner/test_executor.py: budget breach — canned script with excessive turns → status=budget_exceeded
tests/runner/test_executor.py: agent_stuck — canned script that emits only plain text for 4 turns → status=failed reason='agent_stuck'

tests/runner/test_executor.py: pre-action gate catches an un-flagged destructive action — canned script tries to click 'Send' on a step the skill didn't flag → pre-action gate forces confirmation → test submits abort → status=aborted
tests/runner/test_executor.py: dry-run vs execute mode — both use the same executor, differ only in which input_adapter/screen_source are passed in
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the integration point for everything else in the module. The test coverage here is what gives us confidence the module works end-to-end. The 'agent_stuck' recovery prevents a model failure mode where it just narrates without acting — worth failing fast rather than burning tokens.

userStories.title: Kill switch implementation with 2-second abort guarantee
userStories.description: As the user, when I press the global kill-switch hotkey during a run, the run must abort within 2 seconds: the in-flight Anthropic API request is cancelled, any pending confirmation is submitted as abort, the RunWriter flushes final state, and the input adapter is no longer used.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/kill_switch.py exposes KillSwitch class with per-run kill events (asyncio.Event) and a global kill(run_id, reason) method
Executor's async run() is wrapped in an asyncio.shield + asyncio.wait for the kill event — kill cancels the current turn at the next await point
The Anthropic AsyncClient call is cancellable — uses asyncio.create_task + cancel, resulting in an httpx request cancellation
After cancellation, executor: pushes abort decision to any pending confirmation, writer.update_status(aborted, abort_reason=reason), writer.close(), returns
Total time from kill() invocation to status=aborted written to disk: < 2 seconds in 95% of cases; < 5 seconds in 99% (slack for in-flight HTTP)
kill() is idempotent — calling twice is safe
kill() before the run has started is a no-op (logged)
kill() after the run has finished is a no-op (logged)
The kill signal is surfaced via the API: POST /run/{run_id}/abort triggers KillSwitch.kill
The Tauri global hotkey handler calls POST /run/{run_id}/abort via HTTP
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py (fake mode): kill during a long canned script aborts within 2 seconds (measured with time.monotonic)
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: kill while awaiting confirmation → abort decision pushed, status=aborted
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: kill before start → no-op, no error
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: kill after finish → no-op
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: idempotent kill (call twice) does not double-transition status
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: kill cancels the in-flight httpx request (verified via respx delayed response + cancel)
tests/runner/test_kill_switch.py: after kill, the executor does NOT dispatch any further tool calls (verified by inspecting DryRunInputAdapter's recorded calls — no new calls after kill time)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The 2-second target comes from CLAUDE.md safety invariants. The main risk is a blocking PyObjC call in the input adapter that can't be interrupted — mitigation is that the adapter is only called between turns, so we can check the kill signal before each adapter call.

userStories.title: FastAPI routes for run lifecycle and run listing
userStories.description: As the Tauri frontend, I need HTTP endpoints to start a run, stream status updates via WebSocket, submit confirmation decisions, abort a run, list historical runs, and fetch a specific run's detail, so the UI can drive execution and display history.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/api.py exposes a FastAPI APIRouter mounted at /run and /runs on the gateway

POST /run/start body {skill_slug, parameters, mode: 'execute'|'dry_run'} → {run_id}; 404 if skill not found; 400 if mode=execute and TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE is not set (refuse to start live runs without the flag)
GET /run/{run_id} → current run_metadata.json contents

GET /run/{run_id}/stream → WebSocket; messages: {type: 'status_change', status}, {type: 'turn_complete', turn_number, token_totals}, {type: 'confirmation_request', ...}, {type: 'event', ...}, {type: 'done', final_metadata}
POST /run/{run_id}/confirm body {decision: 'confirm'|'abort'} → {accepted: true}; 409 if not awaiting_confirmation; 404 if unknown run
POST /run/{run_id}/abort (no body) → {aborted: true}; always succeeds (idempotent)
GET /runs → list of run summaries from index.db: {run_id, skill_slug, status, started_at, ended_at, duration_seconds, total_cost_usd}, newest first, limit 50 by default
GET /runs?skill_slug=X → filter by skill
GET /run/{run_id}/events → returns events.jsonl contents as JSON array (for debugging / transcript view)
GET /run/{run_id}/screenshots/{filename} → serves PNG from the run's screenshots/ directory
Binds to 127.0.0.1 ONLY
tests/runner/test_api.py: start → stream → confirm → done happy path
tests/runner/test_api.py: start with mode=execute and TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE unset → 400 with message about the flag
tests/runner/test_api.py: abort an active run → 200, run transitions to aborted
tests/runner/test_api.py: abort an already-finished run → 200 (idempotent)
tests/runner/test_api.py: confirm on a run that's not awaiting → 409
tests/runner/test_api.py: GET /runs returns newest first
tests/runner/test_api.py: WebSocket sends keepalive ping every 15s
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE check at the API layer is a defense-in-depth — the adapter's __init__ also checks it, but rejecting at the API layer gives the user a clearer error message than a 500.

userStories.title: Run index SQLite for fast list queries
userStories.description: As the API, I need a SQLite index of runs so GET /runs is fast and doesn't have to scan every run directory on disk.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/run_index.py exposes RunIndex class backed by the shared SQLite at ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/index.db

Schema: runs(run_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, skill_slug TEXT, status TEXT, mode TEXT, started_at TEXT, ended_at TEXT, duration_seconds REAL, total_cost_usd REAL, final_step_reached INTEGER, abort_reason TEXT)
RunWriter inserts a row on construction (status=pending) and updates it on every status change
reconcile() method scans the runs/ directory and rebuilds the index
Startup of the runner service runs reconcile() once
Indexed by (started_at DESC) for fast recency queries
Indexed by skill_slug for filter queries
tests/runner/test_run_index.py: insert, update, list, filter-by-slug, reconcile
tests/runner/test_run_index.py: reconcile handles a crash-mid-run (a run directory exists but its last status transition wasn't flushed) — marks as 'failed' with reason='incomplete_on_restart'
tests/runner/test_run_index.py: pagination (list with limit, offset)
tests/runner/test_run_index.py: list ordering — newest started_at first
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Sharing index.db with the recorder and synthesizer is fine — SQLite handles concurrent reads well and the write volume is low. Each service owns its own tables.

userStories.title: Integration test: full execution pipeline end-to-end in dry-run (fake-mode)
userStories.description: As a confidence check, I need an integration test that drives the entire runner pipeline in dry_run mode with fake-mode LLM — from POST /run/start through WebSocket streaming, confirmation handling, and final run metadata — for each of the 5 reference skills, so the whole pipeline is exercised without real API or real input.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/runner/test_e2e_fake.py runs the full pipeline for each of the 5 fixture skills

For each fixture: starts a dry_run via POST /run/start, connects to WebSocket, walks through the agent turns (canned fake-mode script), submits a 'confirm' decision when the executor hits a destructive step, asserts the run completes with status=succeeded
Asserts run_metadata.json is valid and ended_at is set
Asserts transcript.jsonl has at least as many turns as the canned script
Asserts events.jsonl contains 'step_start' for each skill step
Asserts events.jsonl contains 'confirmation_requested' for each destructive step and 'confirmed' after each confirm decision
Asserts screenshots/ has PNGs for each turn that included a screenshot tool call
Asserts cost totals in costs.jsonl sum to the total in run_metadata.total_cost_usd (within $0.001)
Asserts zero real API calls made (respx catches any attempted network traffic)
Asserts zero real input events posted (DryRunInputAdapter's recorded calls match expectations; no LiveInputAdapter instantiated)
tests/runner/test_e2e_fake.py: abort flow — start a run, abort mid-way via POST /run/{id}/abort, assert status=aborted and no further tool calls after abort time
tests/runner/test_e2e_fake.py: kill switch flow — same as above but via the kill switch API path
tests/runner/test_e2e_fake.py: budget breach flow — canned script with 150 turns → status=budget_exceeded

tests/runner/test_e2e_fake.py: pre-action gate false-negative test — canned script issues a click on an AX element labeled 'Send' for a step the skill didn't flag → gate forces confirmation → test submits abort → status=aborted
Each full e2e run completes in under 15 seconds (no real LLM latency)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is where the module gets its 'dry-run Ralph completion' signal. All 5 reference skills completing end-to-end in fake-mode is the strongest available signal short of real hardware. The pre-action gate test is specifically designed to catch a class of bug that the prompt-level gate alone could miss.

userStories.title: Snapshot test: replay correctness against reference trajectories
userStories.description: As a semantic-equivalence check, I need a test that compares each fake-mode run's sequence of dispatched actions against the source trajectory's click/type events — the runner should roughly reproduce the workflow — so we have confidence the fake-mode scripts are realistic and the parser/dispatcher combo doesn't drift from what was recorded.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/runner/test_replay_correctness.py for each of the 5 reference skills
For each: loads the source trajectory, extracts the sequence of click targets and text inputs, runs the full e2e execution in dry-run mode, inspects DryRunInputAdapter.get_recorded_calls
Compares the dispatched actions to the trajectory's semantic events — allowing for some slack (agent may take extra screenshots, minor ordering differences within a step)

Asserts: (a) every non-destructive click in the trajectory corresponds to a dispatched click (by target label, not coordinates), (b) every text input in the trajectory corresponds to a dispatched type_text, (c) every destructive click in the trajectory became a confirmation request
Uses a semantic-equivalence helper function rather than strict equality — trajectory 'clicked Send button' corresponds to dispatched click at the 'Send' button's coordinates even if the coords differ
Reports per-skill metrics: {clicks_matched, text_inputs_matched, confirmations_fired, extra_screenshots, missing_actions} — prints as part of pytest output
Pass threshold: ≥ 80% clicks matched AND 100% text_inputs matched AND 100% confirmations fired for destructive steps
Fails with a clear diff if below threshold: 'Expected clicks: [...]; Actual dispatched: [...]'
tests/runner/test_replay_correctness.py: all 5 fixtures pass the threshold
Makes use of the trajectory_reader module (imported from synthesizer package)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This test is the counterpart to the synthesizer's similarity test — rather than comparing two skill files, it compares a run against a trajectory. It catches regressions where the fake-mode scripts drift from realistic agent behavior.

userStories.title: Cost budget and alerting for runner
userStories.description: As a safety measure, I need per-run and daily cost budgets with alerts, so a stuck agent or a runaway retry loop doesn't quietly rack up bills — especially critical because the runner can run many times per day.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/src/runner/budget_config.py exposes load_runner_budget() that reads ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/config.json for {run_per_execution_usd_cap: float (default 2.00), run_daily_usd_cap: float (default 20.00)}
Per-run cap is enforced via the existing BudgetTracker (X-014) — max cost translated to max tokens using the pricing table from the LLM client
Per-run exceeded → status=budget_exceeded, abort_reason='per_run_cost_cap'
Daily cap is enforced at POST /run/start — reads costs.jsonl, sums today's runner entries, returns 429 if over daily cap
Logs a stderr warning at 80% of either cap
UI receives the warning via a 'warning' WebSocket message so it can display a banner
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: per-run cap triggers abort
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: daily cap triggers 429 on start
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: config.json missing → defaults used
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: invalid config → ConfigurationError on load
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: 80% warning fires at the right threshold
tests/runner/test_budget_config.py: dev profile uses lower caps (runner_per_execution_usd_cap=0.50, runner_daily_usd_cap=2.00 are the dev defaults)
Ralph iterations have the lower dev-profile caps in effect by default (so Ralph can't accidentally spend > $2/day on runner iterations)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The runner's budget is tighter than the synthesizer's because runs happen more often (synthesis is ~once per skill; runs happen every time the skill is used).

userStories.title: AGENTS.md for runner module and CLAUDE.md update
userStories.description: As project maintenance, I need services/runner/AGENTS.md with module-specific context and any needed CLAUDE.md updates, so future Ralph iterations (this branch or cross-branch) benefit from discovered patterns.
acceptanceCriteria: services/runner/AGENTS.md exists with sections: Purpose, Key files, Safety model (three-layer destructive gate), Dry-run vs live mode, Common gotchas, How to run tests, How live testing works

Safety model section explicitly enumerates the three layers: (1) skill ⚠️ flags → prompt instructions → <needs_confirmation>, (2) parser ignores tool_use when confirmation tag is present, (3) pre-action gate inspects AX labels at click time
Dry-run vs live mode section documents TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE semantics, which adapters use which flag, and the exact command to enable live mode for manual testing
Common gotchas section includes at least 5 concrete lessons from Ralph iteration (CGEventPost uses points not pixels, PyObjC return-tuple gotchas, httpx cancellation behavior, etc.)
How live testing works section describes the X-025 live checklist as a pre-merge gate, NOT a Ralph completion gate
CLAUDE.md at repo root updated with any cross-module runner notes
progress.txt has appended entries per Ralph iteration that discovered something non-trivial
AGENTS.md references CLAUDE.md: 'See /CLAUDE.md for project-wide context, safety invariants, and reference workflows.'
AGENTS.md length is 150-500 lines
No secrets, API keys, or user data in any committed file
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the autonomous-phase handoff. After Ralph flips all X-001..X-024 to passes:true and emits RUNNER_DRY_RUN_DONE, a human picks up with X-025.

userStories.title: Human live-execution checklist (blocks PR-to-main, NOT Ralph completion)
userStories.description: As the pre-merge gate for this branch, I need a structured checklist that a human runs on a real machine with TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1 against disposable test accounts, executing each of the 5 reference skills live and verifying correct behavior, so no untested real-world execution ever reaches main. This story does NOT block Ralph's completion promise — Ralph flips passes:true on X-001..X-024 and stops; X-025 is flipped by a human after live testing.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/runner/live_checklist.md exists and is NOT mutated by Ralph iterations (a note at top: 'HUMAN-EDITED FILE — Ralph MUST NOT modify this')

Checklist section per reference workflow, each with checkboxes: [ ] Created disposable test account / sandbox for this workflow (test Gmail, test Slack, etc.), [ ] Ran POST /run/start with mode=execute and TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1, [ ] Observed execution on screen, [ ] Confirmation prompt fired correctly for each destructive step, [ ] Clicked Confirm and the destructive step executed correctly, [ ] Clicked Abort on one dry test — verified abort within 2 seconds, [ ] Kill-switch hotkey test — verified abort within 2 seconds, [ ] Final state of target app matches skill's Expected outcome section
Per-workflow issues log: space for the human tester to note anomalies (misclicks, timing issues, AX resolution failures)
Cost sanity check: total cost of all 5 live runs documented; assert under $5 total
Sign-off section at the end: human tester name, date, macOS version, hardware (M-series vs Intel), test account(s) used
A 'known issues that are NOT blockers' subsection: document Retina-scale quirks, specific AX coverage holes on Chrome/Electron, other oddities — these can be logged without blocking merge
A 'blockers' subsection: any issue here BLOCKS the PR merge
CLAUDE.md's Runner section references this checklist explicitly as the pre-merge gate

README.md in services/runner documents the full pre-merge procedure: set TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1, use the dev profile, use disposable test accounts, fill out the checklist, attach signed checklist to the PR description
scripts/check_live_signoff.sh exists and parses live_checklist.md to verify the sign-off section is filled in; exits 0 if signed, 1 if not — used by the PR template / CI to block merge
X-025's passes flag is NOT modified by the Ralph loop — the Ralph prompt.md / CLAUDE.md at branch level instructs Ralph to skip this story entirely
Ralph completion promise RUNNER_DRY_RUN_DONE fires when X-001 through X-024 all have passes:true, regardless of X-025
userStories.notes: This is the most important safety story in the whole project. The split — Ralph completes autonomously on dry-run unit tests, a human gates the merge on live execution — is the compromise we discussed. Ralph's prompt must be explicit: 'X-025 is human-only. Do NOT modify it. Do NOT flip its passes flag. Emit RUNNER_DRY_RUN_DONE when X-001..X-024 are all passes:true.'

scope: Claude Sonnet 4.5 as the only v1 agent runtime (OpenAI/Hermes stubs are placeholder)
Dry-run and live modes only — no 'simulation' or 'record-and-replay' mode in v1
Parameterization at run time is string substitution only (no conditional logic, no loops)
No shell execution by the agent — computer_20250124 is the ONLY tool granted

performance.turnLatency: Dominated by LLM latency (Sonnet 4.5 for computer use is ~2-5s per turn); harness overhead < 300 ms per turn
performance.killSwitch: < 2s from hotkey to status=aborted on disk (95th percentile)
performance.apiEndpointNonLlm: < 100 ms p50 for list / get / delete

resources.timeline: 2-3 weeks for this module (highest-risk, most surface area)
resources.teamSize: 1 (solo, Ralph-assisted for dry-run; human-driven for X-025)
resources.apiCostBudgetForRalph: $10 total across all Ralph iterations — fake-mode covers 99% of tests
resources.apiCostBudgetForLiveSmoke: $5 total for the X-025 live checklist

aiLlm: Fake-mode auto-enabled in pytest via conftest.py fixture
Real API calls only in integration branch (Tauri UI) or X-025 manual execution
Retry policy: exponential backoff on 429/5xx, max 5 retries; 401 fails fast
Per-run and daily cost caps enforced (X-023)

security.network: FastAPI binds to 127.0.0.1 ONLY. Anthropic API calls go to api.anthropic.com over HTTPS. No other network destinations. WebSocket is local-only.
security.filesystem: Reads skills under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/skills/ (read-only). Writes runs under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/runs/ with dir perms 0700. No writes outside this tree.
security.secrets: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY read from environment only. Never logged. Never written to disk. Never returned in any API response.
security.dataHandling: Screenshots from live runs are sent to Anthropic. They may contain sensitive on-screen data. Documented trade-off in README.
security.destructiveGateArchitecture: Three independent layers: (1) skill metadata flags ⚠️ destructive steps → prompt instructs agent to emit <needs_confirmation> → executor pauses and waits for user; (2) parser prioritizes <needs_confirmation> over any tool_use in the same turn — defense against agent emitting both; (3) pre-action AX inspection on every click-class tool call matches keyword list against target label — forces confirmation even if skill didn't flag and agent didn't signal.
ralphGuardrails: TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE must never be set during Ralph iterations
Live input and live screen adapters refuse to instantiate without TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1 (X-001 gate)
POST /run/start refuses mode=execute without TRACE_ALLOW_LIVE=1 (X-019 gate)
Ralph iterations use the dev profile: ~/Library/Application Support/Trace-dev/
Ralph iterations use a SEPARATE ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (the dev key) with a $20/month cap in Anthropic console
Daily cost cap (X-023) is $2 in the dev profile vs $20 in prod
Ralph iterations skip X-025 entirely — the Ralph prompt and CLAUDE.md enforce this

nonGoals: OpenAI or Hermes agent runtime (stubs only in v1)
Cross-app atomic undo (if a run misclicks, user undoes manually)
Automatic retry of failed runs (user re-runs manually)
Real-time coaching (user watches and corrects mid-run) — abort + retry is the model
Audio cues from the agent (Claude doesn't narrate audibly)
Video recording of runs (only screenshots per turn)

successMetrics.dryRunCompletion: All 5 reference skills complete end-to-end in dry-run fake-mode with status=succeeded in test_e2e_fake
successMetrics.destructiveGateCorrectness: test_executor.py's pre-action-gate-catches-unflagged-destructive test passes — gate catches at least one class of LLM-emitted destructive action not flagged in the skill
successMetrics.replayFidelity: test_replay_correctness passes at ≥ 80% click match on all 5 fixtures
successMetrics.contractStability: contracts/run-metadata.schema.json and contracts/destructive_keywords.json unchanged after respective stories merged
successMetrics.costPerRun: Average dry-run fake-mode cost per reference skill < $0.05 (fake mode is near-free)
successMetrics.cleanQualityGates: ruff, mypy --strict, pytest, scripts/check_contracts.sh all pass at every commit
successMetrics.humanLiveSignoff: X-025 live checklist sign-off completed and passing on all 5 reference workflows before PR-to-main merge (NOT a Ralph completion criterion)

openQuestions: Pre-action gate false-positive rate on real workflows — will it fire too often on 'Share' or 'Confirm' buttons that aren't truly destructive (e.g., 'Share your screen' in a video call)? Defer to X-025 data.
Retina off-by-one edges — CGEventPost post-rounding on fractional coordinates may behave differently on M-series vs Intel. Flag for X-025 testing on both hardware types if possible.
AX resolution latency on Electron apps — 200 ms timeout may be too aggressive for some apps; revisit after X-025.
Run history retention — runs pile up over time; add a 'delete runs older than N days' setting in v2.
Should the agent get conversation memory across multi-run sequences (e.g., learn from prior failures)? No in v1 — each run is independent. Flag for v2.
Confirmation timeout of 5 minutes — too short for users who step away; too long for users who forget. Defer to user feedback.
Whether to fail fast on a specific class of AX error vs retry — currently we just log and continue. May need tuning.
Whether X-025 should be repeated periodically (e.g., monthly) to catch regressions from OS updates — flag as a maintenance process.
